coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-integrated ratiometric fluorescent sensor for ascorbic acid determination using microplasma-enabled carbon dots and rhodamine B
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yilan
Jiang, Xue
Huang, Ke
Xiong, Xiaoli
Yang, Qing
coredata.dc:description: A switchable ratiometric fluorescent smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and Rhodamine B was fabricated for the determination of the ascorbic acid (AA) content in fruits by quenching the fluorescence of N-CDs with Hg2+ (turn-off) and recovering with AA (turn-on). The blue-emission N-CDs was synthesized by liquid dielectric barrier discharge microplasma with an average size of 3.65 nm and an absolute quantum yield of 18 % (excited at 345 nm). In addition, the fluorescence color was converted to RGB values, enabling visual and quantitative determination of AA. Under optimal parameters, the linear ranges for detecting AA were found to be 3–170 μM and 5–170 μM for fluorescence spectrometer and smartphone sensing platform. The detection limits were 0.98 μM and 2.90 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the satisfactory recoveries in fruits were obtained by RF probe and smartphone platform. This smartphone-assisted platform will facilitate sensitive and visual determination for AA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microplasma
Ratiometric fluorescence
Smartphone platform
Ascorbic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and AA using the RF probe
Measuring Hg2+ and AA with smartphone
Selectivity and anti-interference of RF probe
Analysis of AA in food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-CDs; Inset: The particle diameter distribution of N-CDs. (b) HR-TEM image of N-CDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs. (d) XRD patterns of N-CDs. (e) XPS full spectrum of N-CDs, High-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (f), N 1 s (g) and O 1 s (h).

(a) Excitation, emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs. (b) Fluorescent spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 310–390 nm. (c) Fluorescent spectra of N-CDs, RhB, N-CDs@RhB.

(a) Influence of buffer compositions on the determination of Hg2+ and AA (all buffer concentrations were 10 mM). (b) Influence of pH on Hg2+ determination. (c) Influence of pH on the determination of AA. (d) The effect of the temperature on the determination of Hg2+ and AA. (e) Influence of incubation time on the determination of Hg2+ and AA. (f) The influence of Hg2+ concentration on AA determination, R and R0 represented I436/I578 with and without analyte, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the RF probe at various concentrations of Hg2+. (b) Plots of the values of F436/F578 with Hg2+ concentrations. Inset showed the linear relationship with Hg2+ concentration over the range of 5–170 μM. (c) Plots of the values of R/(R + B + G) with the concentrations of Hg2+. The inset showed the linear relationship between R/(R + B + G) with concentrations of Hg2+ over the range of 5–170 μM. (d) The selectivity (blue bars) and anti-interference (red bars) of N-CDs@RhB to other ions (500 μM) on the determination of Hg2+ (200 μM). (0, control; 1, Ba2+; 2, Mg2+; 3, K+; 4, Na+; 5, Zn2+; 6, Al3+; 7, Ca2+; 8, Fe3+; 9, Ni2+; 10, Co2+; 11, CO3 2−; 12, NO2 −; 13, PO4 3−; 14, Ac−; 15, NH4 +.) (e) The fluorescence images at various concentrations of Hg2+ under a 365 nm UV lamp. (f) Fluorescent photos of the RF probe upon addition different analytes under a 365 nm UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the RF probe with Hg2+ (200 μM) upon addition of various concentrations of AA. (b) Plots of the values of F0/F versus AA concentrations. Inset showed the linear relationship on AA determination over the range of 3–170 μM. (c) Plots of the values of R/(R + B + G) versus AA concentration. Inset showed the linear relationship with concentrations of AA over the range of 5–170 μM. (d) Selectivity (blue bars) and anti-interference (red bars) of amino acids and ions (500 μM) on the determination of AA (200 μM). (0, control; 1, Leu; 2, Phe; 3, Thr; 4, Tyr; 5, Val; 6, His; 7, Met; 8, Ser; 9, Gly; 10, Glu; 11, Pro; 12, Arg; 13, Asp; 14, CA; 15, Urea; 16, Ca2+; 17, Na+; 18, Ba2+; 19, Mg2+; 20, K+; 21, NH4 +; 22, Fe3+; 23, PO4 3−; 24, NO2 −.) (e) Corresponding photograph fluorescence images at different concentrations of AA under a 365 nm UV lamp. (f) Fluorescent photos of the RF probe upon addition different analytes under a 365 nm UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthetic route of N-CDs by DBD, and the visual determination principle for AA based RF and smartphone-integrated portable platform.
Determination results of ascorbic acid in fruit samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
. Mean ± SD (n = 3). b. Certified value: 100 μg/mL. c. Critical value t
0.05,4 = 2.78.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-integrated ratiometric fluorescent sensor for ascorbic acid determination using microplasma-enabled carbon dots and rhodamine B

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A switchable ratiometric fluorescent smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and Rhodamine B was fabricated for the determination of the ascorbic acid (AA) content in fruits by quenching the fluorescence of N-CDs with Hg2+ (turn-off) and recovering with AA (turn-on). The blue-emission N-CDs was synthesized by liquid dielectric barrier discharge microplasma with an average size of 3.65 nm and an absolute quantum yield of 18 % (excited at 345 nm). In addition, the fluorescence color was converted to RGB values, enabling visual and quantitative determination of AA. Under optimal parameters, the linear ranges for detecting AA were found to be 3–170 μM and 5–170 μM for fluorescence spectrometer and smartphone sensing platform. The detection limits were 0.98 μM and 2.90 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the satisfactory recoveries in fruits were obtained by RF probe and smartphone platform. This smartphone-assisted platform will facilitate sensitive and visual determination for AA.

The Fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized by liquid-phase dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) microplasma from urea and citric acid in DMF solution. A smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor was constructed based on N-CDs and RhB for “on-off-on” determination of ascorbic acid (AA) by observing the fluorescence color change.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is a water-soluble strong antioxidant that can reduce the damage of reactive oxygen species to proteins, lipids and DNA (Cheng et al., 2018). Besides, AA can directly participate in the synthesis of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), dopamine, catecholamines, etc. (Novoa-De León et al., 2019). As an essential micronutrient, AA can only be obtained through diet such as fresh fruits and vegetables (Luo et al., 2018). However, excessive AA intake can easily lead to gastric irritation, diarrhea and urinary tract stones (Zhu, Zhao, Nie, Liu, & Yao, 2015). Hence, it is crucial to establish effective approaches for quantifying AA in fresh fruits.

So far, various techniques have been devised for AA determination, including capillary electrophoresis (Costa et al., 2019), chromatography (Turak, Güzel, & Dinç, 2017), spectrophotometry (Gómez Ruiz, Roux, Courtois, & Bonazzi, 2016), electrochemistry (Mazzara et al., 2021), colorimetry (Fan, Zhao, Ding, Li, & Chen, 2017) and fluorescence (Pei et al., 2024). Among them, fluorescence analysis has received widespread interest for its advantages of simple equipment, easy manipulation, cost-effectiveness, fast response as well as visualization. Particularly, the ratiometric fluorescent (RF) probe with internal self-calibration function can effectively avoid influences from environmental factors and equipment efficiency (Castro, Ribeiro, & Santos, 2021). More importantly, the RF probe is accompanied with apparent color changes, enabling rapid visual sensing applications. Moreover, smartphone can convert the color information of fluorescent photos into digital signal, helping sensor achieve visual and quantitative determination of various analytes. Combining RF probes with smartphone-assisted vision facilitated achieving sensitive, fast and visual determination.

In this work, we developed a RF probe with the assistance of a portable smartphone platform for visual and rapid determination of AA through the “on-off-on” process, as demonstrated in Scheme 1 . The RF sensor was composed of red-light emitting Rhodamine B (RhB) as a signal reference and blue-light emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as a responsive unit. N-CDs was synthesized using liquid-phase dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) within 1 h. DBD was a type of plasma characterized by high electron density and remarkable stability. This plasma was capable of supplying ample energy for the rapid synthesis of materials, involving metal-organic frameworks and covalent organic frameworks, at atmospheric pressure (J. He et al., 2022). To our knowledge, there were few reports on the research and application of CDs synthesized by DBD method (He et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2023). There are sufficient functional groups on the surface of the prepared N-CDs, which were coordinated with Hg2+, and thus, the fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched. Subsequently, AA was bound to Hg2+, leading to the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs. In addition, the fluorescence color changes were recorded by a smartphone and transformed to RGB values. Under optimal detection conditions, the sensor was used for the determination of AA in fruits. The determination results of several real samples by the proposed method were also compared with that obtained by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with satisfactory consistency and reliability. The constructed RF probe, combined with a smartphone, provided a convenient, fast and visual quantitative determination of AA.

All chemical reagents were analytical pure and used without further purification. Citric acid (CA, C6H8O7), Urea (CON2H4), Co(NO3)2·6H2O, CH3COONa, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Ba(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Ni(NO3)3·6H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, NaCl, NH4Cl, NaNO2, Na2CO3, NaOH, NaH2PO4, Rhodamine B (RhB), ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys), leucine (Leu), arginine (Arg) and N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) solution were purchased from Chengdu Kelon Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) and glutathione (GSH) were bought from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). phenylalanine (Phe), threonine (Thr), tyrosin (Tyr), valine (Val), histidine (His), methionine (Met), glutamic (Glu), aspartic acid (Asp) and proline (Pro) were purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Tris (hydroxymethyl) methyl aminomethane (99 %) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). High purity deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm) from a water purification system (Chengdu Ultrapure Technology Co.; Ltd., Chengdu, China) was used in all experiments.

The DBD reactor consisted of open concentric quartz tube, nickel rod, copper wire and a transformer TDGC2–1 (CHNT, China). The Rigaku/MAX2550 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA) (λ = 1.5418 nm) (Rigaku, Japan) was used to acquire X-ray diffraction (XRD) data. An ESCALABMK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer by Mg-Kα X-ray was used to acquire X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. XPSPeak (Informer Technologies, Inc.) was used for spectra deconvolution. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) spectra were measured using a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, America). A Bruker Optics VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) was used to conduct Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra. The UV absorption spectra were studied by Techcomp UV2310II spectrophotometer (Techcomp, China). Fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yield were recorded with an Edinburgh FLS980 Steady-state/Lifetime spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, England). The hydrodynamic diameter of the materials was obtained by Zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZSP, Malvern, England). HPLC measurements were carried out by an Agilent 1290 (Agilent, USA). All of the fluorescent spectra were conducted in a 1 cm path length quartz cuvette with an RF-6000 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The slit widths of excitation and emission were 3 nm and 5 nm, respectively. Fluorescent color photographs were taken in a ZF-8 Black-box UV analyzer (Jiapeng, China) with a power of 6 W using a smartphone (Redmi K50, China), and RGB was extracted by color recognition extractor of the software (ImageJ, National Institutes of Health).

A modified method was adopted to prepare N-CDs (Li et al., 2020). In brief, CA (1.2 g) and urea (1.2 g) were dissolved into 10 mL DMF solvent. After 10 min of sonication, the mixture was placed into a DBD tube and reacted at 90 V for 60 min. After cooling, the prepared N-CDs solution was kept at the refrigerator (4 °C). In addition, the important parameters such as voltage, reaction time of the system and the ratio between citric acid and urea were systematically tested to optimize the performance of N-CDs.

140 μL CDs solution, 100 μL RhB (0.05 mg/mL) solution, 300 μL Hg2+ at different concentrations were mixed with 1.5 mL citrate phosphate buffer solution (CPB, 10 mM CA, 20 mM Na2HPO4, pH = 6) and filled up to 3 mL with ultrapure water. Following a 40-min shaking period at room temperature, the fluorescent spectra were measured (the excitation wavelength was 345 nm). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I436/I578) between N-CDs (I436) and RhB (I578) was used as the signal output value for Hg2+ concentration analysis.

140 μL CDs solution, 100 μL RhB solution, 300 μL Hg2+ (200 μM) were successively added into 1.5 mL CPB buffer solution, then 300 μL of the AA standard solution at various concentrations were also added. The solution was finally diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water. Following a 20-min shaking period, the fluorescent spectra were measured and the concentration of AA was quantitatively analyzed by the fluorescence intensity ratio (I436/I578). Moreover, for visualization determination, different colors were recorded and then converted to RGB values by color recognition extractor of the software.

Seven distinct categories of fruit samples and beverages with various AA levels were selected to assess the practicability of the suggested technique. The extraction of AA from each fruit was performed as described in the literature (Wang et al., 2020). Fruits were peeled and squeezed into juice. The obtained juice were centrifuged at a speed of 8000 rpm for 5 min and subsequently leached through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Before testing, the juice was diluted to the proper consistency with the ultrapure water (10-fold dilution for orange, lemon and citrus juice, 20-fold dilution for pear and tomato juice, 30-fold dilution for beverages). All fruit samples and beverages were bought from the local supermarket (Sichuan Province, China). Samples were analyzed with the steps mentioned above.

All data were performed in triplicate and expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). All graphical data were plotted and analyzed using Origin 9.65 software (Microcal, USA). Different significance tests between the fluorescence method and the smartphone-assisted method, as well as the proposed method and HPLC method were subjected to a student's test (t-test) at 95 % confidence interval.

In this study, N-CDs was synthesized by the DBD method. During the process, the synthesis effect of N-CDs could be affected by various factors, such as ratio of carbon and nitrogen source, voltage as well as reaction time, which had been optimized. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the optimal parameters were determined as follows: a mass ratio of carbon and nitrogen sources 1:1, a voltage of 90 V and a time of 60 min.

The morphology of the as-synthesized N-CDs was manifested by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and HR-TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, N-CDs possessed a nearly spherical shape and was finely scattered in the water phase without apparent aggregation. The particle sizes were mostly in the scope of 1–7 nm and the average size was 3.65 nm. No obvious signature of fringe was found in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1b), suggesting the N-CDs had amorphous nature, as other researchers had reported (Chen, Liu, Li, & Huang, 2019). The X-ray diffractogram (XRD) of N-CDs displayed a broad peak at 28° (Fig. 1d), a characteristic carbonaceous material, implying that the N-CDs were amorphous and highly disordered (Zhang et al., 2019).

The fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was analyzed to identify the surface functional groups of N-CDs. The absorption peaks at 3413 and 3303 cm−1 were induced by OH and NH stretching vibrations (Fig. 1c). The peaks of 1671 and 1602 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibrations of the CO bond in the carboxylic acid (-COOH) group and C(=O)NH2, demonstrating the possible existence of -COOH and C(=O)NH2 in N-CDs (Yuefang Hu et al., 2017) (W. Zhang et al., 2016). The absorption peak at 1403 cm−1 was formed from the vibration of CN. Besides, the peak at 1030 cm−1 was put down to the stretching vibration of C-O-C (He et al., 2020).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of N-CDs were illustrated in Fig. 1 (e-h). Three significant peaks at 284, 399.3, and 532.6 eV were correlated with C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s respectively. The C 1 s peaks (Fig. 1f) at 284.8, 285.9, and 287.9 eV represented CC, C-OH, and O-C=O, respectively (Han et al., 2024). The spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 1g) displayed the characteristic peaks of CN, NH, and C3 N at 399.5, 399.9 and 400.7 eV. The peaks of O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 1h could be ascribed to CO (531.8 eV), C-OH (532.6 eV), CO (533.41 eV) and surface adsorbed water molecules (535.7 eV), respectively (Pattnayak, Sahoo, Choudhury, & Hota, 2022). XPS and FT-IR analysis further confirmed that N-CD surfaces was covered with abundant carboxyl and amino groups.

UV–vis spectrum and fluorescent excitation/emission spectra of N-CDs were shown at Fig. 2a. The maximum UV absorption peak at 338 nm was characterized as the n-π* transition of the CO, CN and CN bonds (Han et al., 2024). The maximal fluorescence intensity was noticed at 436 nm when exciting at 345 nm. Fig. 2b indicated the excitation-dependent behavior of N-CDs. The emission wavelength was progressively red-shifted from 430 nm to 460 nm, when the excitation wavelength was changed from 310 nm to 390 nm. This result was caused by the inhomogeneity of the particles and the surface state that affected the band gap of N-CDs (Kalaiyarasan & Joseph, 2017). In addition, Fig. S2 revealed that the as-prepared N-CDs featured superior fluorescence properties with an absolute quantum yield of 18.87 %.

In order to excite N-CDs and RhB simultaneously, 345 nm was picked to be the optimal excitation wavelength for sensing. The RF sensor exhibited two fluorescence peaks at 436 nm and 578 nm, which were attributed to the emission peaks of N-CDs and RhB, respectively (Fig. 2c). Therefore, a RF probe was successfully constructed.

Experimental parameters were optimized to attain the best sensing performance for Hg2+ and AA determination, including the ratio of N-CDs and RhB, buffer composition, pH value, reaction temperature and incubation time.

For a wider color variation, the fluorescence intensity ratio of N-CDs and RhB (I436/I578) was optimized by varying the concentration of N-CDs (Fig. S3). The best fluorescence ratio for visual assay was observed when I436/I578 was 5:1. Fig. 3a indicated that the original fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was essentially unchanged in various buffer compositions. Nevertheless, the optimal efficiency on fluorescence quenching and recovery were observed in CPB. Therefore, CPB was picked to be the optimal detection buffer solution for further experiments. The response of the RF probe to Hg2+ and AA at different pH (4–10) was shown in Fig. 3b-c. The maximum fluorescence quenching degree of Hg2+ was observed at pH 6, which could be ascribed to the protonation-deprotonation of -OH and -NH2 functional group (T. Zhang et al., 2019). Under the same pH value, the fluorescence recovery effect caused by AA reached the maximum. These results suggested that pH 6 was the most suitable pH for subsequent experiments. For reaction temperature (25–50 °C, Fig. 3d), no significant change was found for quenching efficiency for Hg2+ determination, while recovery efficiency for AA determination reached a maximum at 25 °C. Thus, the ideal incubation temperature of 25 °C was chosen for Hg2+ and AA determination. The optimal times for the determination of Hg2+ and AA were shown in Fig. 3e. 40-min incubation period was chosen for the determination of Hg2+, and 20-min reaction time was selected for the determination of AA. The influence of Hg2+ concentration for AA determination was also examined. As depicted in Fig. 3f, the most significant response was observed at Hg2+ concentration of 200 μM. Thus, 200 μM Hg2+ was chosen for sensing AA.

The fluorescence intensity of probe remained steady within 1 M NaCl solution (Fig. S4a), indicating excellent salt resistance of the probe. In addition, a systematic investigation was conducted on the stability of this probe within 14 days (Fig. S4b). There was no obvious change in fluorescence ratio, indicating that the RF sensor possessed good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and AA using the RF probe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the impact of various levels of Hg2+ on the RF probe was investigated. As indicated in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 436 nm was progressively decreased, whereas the fluorescence intensity of RhB at 578 nm remained relatively stable. In the range of 5–170 μM, the ratio of fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ (I436/I578 = −0.0237 CHg 2+ + 4.9060) with the square of correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9973. Based on the equation 3ẟ/k, where ẟ represented the standard deviation of 11 blank samples and k denoted the slope of the regression equation, the limit of detection (LOD) of Hg2+ was determined to be 0.99 μM.

Fig. 5a-b displayed the fluorescence signal of the N-CDs@RhB-Hg2+ system for AA determination. With the increase of AA content, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs achieved varying degrees of recovery, while that of RhB remained constant. The value of I436/I578 demonstrated a good linear relationship with the concentration of AA in the range of 3–170 μM, and fitted an equation I436/I578 = 0.0206 CAA + 0.5348 (R2 = 0.9982, LOD = 0.98 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measuring Hg2+ and AA with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the samples with different concentrations of analytes were placed under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation, photographed, and the color was converted to RGB. Finally, the concentration of the analytes was quantified by calculating the ratio of R/(R + G + B). Fig. 4e showed that the mixture solution varied in color from blue to purple to orange-red with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, and the corresponding ratio R/(R + G + B) values also gradually increased which showed a good linear relationship (R/(R + G + B) = 0.0011 CHg 2+ + 0.1523, R2 = 0.9968) over the range of 5–170 μM, as shown in Fig. 4c. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 2.63 μM. For AA determination, the RF probe varied in color from orange-red to purple to blue while the R/(R + G + B) values decreased with increasing AA concentration (Fig. 5c and Fig. 5e). The results indicated that R/(R + G + B) value exhibited good linear correlation with AA concentration of 5–170 μM. The linear regression equation was R/(R + G + B) = − 0.0011 CAA + 0.3855 (R2 = 0.9993). The calculated LOD was 2.90 μM. In addition, theoretical CIE coordinate analysis further confirmed this result. The CIE coordinates of the RF sensor for Hg2+ and AA were shown in Fig. S5a and Fig. S5b, which were similar to the fluorescent photos under 365 nm UV lamp. These results provided a basis and convenience for visual determination of AA using the RF sensor.

The proposed method was compared with some previously reported fluorescence methods for AA determination, as listed in Table. S2. Compared with other techniques, this method used harmful metal Hg2+, but offered a wider linear range and an excellent LOD. In addition, “on-off-on” mode was also beneficial in reducing the possibilities of false positive due to environmental stimulus in the single-step “turn-off” models. Furthermore, integrating fluorescence spectrometer with a smartphone sensing platform enabled the visual analysis of AA, a novel means of AA determination which was portable and intuitive.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of RF probe
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and anti-interference of the sensor for Hg2+ was evaluated via the collection of emission spectra when different kinds of ions were added into the system. Fig. 4d showed that only Hg2+ was able to significantly quench the fluorescence of the sensor, while other cations and anions had negligible effects. Simultaneously, the results of interference test showed that the fluorescence signals and the fluorescence color were unaffected by the co-existing interfering ions.

The selectivity and anti-interference of other amino acids and several ions on N-CDs@RhB-Hg2+ towards AA determination were also tested. As indicated in Fig. 5d, the fluorescence of N-CDs was recovered significantly only with the addition of AA, whereas other ions and amino acids had no such effect. In addition, the results of the anti-interference test showed that the influences of these potential interfering species on the I436/I578 value was 97.9–104.4 % of the blank samples (97.9 % for Fe3+ and 104.4 % for Met), and the interference level was negligible. However, the fluorescence of N-CDs@RhB-Hg2+ can also be restored by glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys). Nevertheless, the interference of GSH and Cys could be suppressed by N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) (Yalei Hu et al., 2017). As demonstrated in Fig. S6, the signal enhancement of N-CDs@RhB-Hg2+ was negligible after a 40-min incubation of GSH (100 μM) or Cys (100 μM) alongside NEM (300 μM), indicating that the interference of Cys and GSH on AA determination could be effectively shielded by NEM. Therefore, the proposed method had excellent anti-external interference ability, which can be used for AA measurement in practical samples.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism is generally divided into dynamic (DQE) and static quenching (SQE), which can be described in theory though the Stern-Volmer equation and the fluorescence lifetime:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ ave
Q
where F
0 and F referred to the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 436 nm without and with Hg2+, respectively. [Q] referred to Hg2+ concentration, K
SV represented the Stern-Volmer constant, τ
ave was set as the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs, and Kq designated the fluorescence quenching rate constant.

As indicated in Table. S1 and Fig. S7, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs reduced from 9.53 ns to 8.41 ns after the addition of Hg2+, which confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Hg2+ was probably through a dynamic process (Zu et al., 2017). The nonlinear relationship was observed when the Stern-Volmer plot was fitted versus Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 5–175 μM (Fig. S8a-b). It was inferred that the fluorescence quenching may be initiated by multiple mechanisms (Bai et al., 2018; Hao et al., 2023). Moreover, the Stern-Volmer equation exhibited good linear behavior in the concentration range of Hg2+ (5–30 μM), with KSV of 2.50 × 103 M−1. Kq was determined to be 2.6 × 1011 M−1 S−1 (>2.0 × 1010 M−1 S−1), demonstrating that the quenching of N-CDs by Hg2+ was considered to be a the SQE process at low concentration (Hao et al., 2023; Xie et al., 2022). These results depicted that the quenching process of N-CDs should be a result of DQE and SQE equilibrium.

As depicted in Fig. S8c, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs exhibited no obvious change with only AA. However, the fluorescence of N-CDs quenched by Hg2+ could be recovered by AA, indicating that AA could interact with Hg2+ or the products of Hg2+ and N-CDs. By incorporating nitrogen into CDs, the electronic density of N-CDs can be efficiently controlled, which would facilitate the coordination interaction between Hg2+ and -COOH/-NH2 on the N-CDs surface. The “on-off-on” fluorescence changes were attributed to the continuous formation and disaggregation of N-CDs-Hg2+ compound (Issa et al., 2022). After the complex formation of N-CDs and Hg2+, non-radiative electrons transferred from N-CDs to Hg2+, resulting in the suppression of the fluorescence (L.-S. Li et al., 2018). Whereas each AA molecule with four -OH could easily bind to Hg2+ and dissociate Hg2+ from the complex, leading to the fluorescence recovery. The UV–vis absorption spectra were also explored to confirm this speculation. The peak of N-CDs at 338 nm weakened and a slight red-shift was observed with the presence of Hg2+ (from 338 nm to 341 nm). The absorption spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. S8d) was then recovered after the addition of AA, indicating the binding between Hg2+ and AA. Furthermore, the absorption peak of AA at 265 nm disappeared with the addition of Hg2+, which may be caused by the reduction of Hg2+ by AA (Wang et al., 2018). Moreover, based on DLS analysis (Fig. S9), the increase of N-CDs particle size after the reaction also proved the possible appearance of N-CDs-Hg2+ compound.

In order to investigate the surface state of N-CDs after contacting with Hg2+, FT-IR (Fig. S10) and XPS (Fig. S11) spectra were researched. The peaks at 1480 cm−1 (CN), 1642 cm−1 (-COOH) and 3100 cm−1 (−NH2) in FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs became weaker after reacting with Hg2+. Four peaks at 537.17, 404.67, 285.01 and 100.33 eV in Fig. S11 were classified as O 1 s, C 1 s, N 1 s and Hg 4 f. Compared with N-CDs, the bond energy and content of O-C=O, NH, and NN had changed after reacting with Hg2+, which was attributed to the coordination of -COOH and -NH2 to Hg2+ on the N-CDs surface.

Based on above analysis, the mechanism of the N-CDs fluorescence quenching by Hg2+ was determined as the combined effect of DQE and SQE. The functional groups -NH2/-COOH of N-CDs may coordinate with Hg2+ to form the N-CDs-Hg2+ complex, causing the fluorescence signal to turn off. AA bound to Hg2+ and Hg2+ was dissociated from N-CDs-Hg2+ complex, resulting in the fluorescence recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AA in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs@RhB-Hg2+ sensor was practically applied for AA determination in fruits by fluorescence detection and smartphone sensing platform in Table. 1 . For fluorescent method, the recovery of AA ranged from 94.0 to 106.9 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 2.2 %. Moreover, the recovery of AA ranged from 94.4 to 105.5 % for smartphone-assisted method with RSD < 5.0 %. In addition, a certified AA standard solution (BWQ9136–2016) was used for further verification, and the validation results of both the fluorescent method and the smartphone-assisted method were in accordance with its certified value. A t-test was also used to assess the differences between fluorescence method and the smartphone-assisted method. The calculated t-values were in the range of 0.3–2.5 (less than the theoretical t0.05,4 = 2.78), indicating that no significant difference was found between fluorescence method and the smartphone-assisted method at 95 % confidence interval. Meanwhile, the differences between the N-CDs@RhB and the conventional HPLC detection method were compared in Table. S3. The calculated t values were between 0.15 and 1.95 (<t0.05,4), which indicated that no significant differences were found between the fluorescence method and the HPLC method, as well as the smartphone-assisted method and the HPLC method, proving excellent accuracy and applicability of the sensor.

In the experiment, all the juices were diluted to the appropriate concentration, and the original concentrations of AA in lemon juice, orange juice, tomato juice, pear juice and citrus juice were calculated to be 5.55 mM, 2.29 mM, 0.23 mM, 0.44 mM and 1.66 mM, respectively. Although the smartphone determination platform exhibited lower precision than the fluorescence analysis, it enabled the visual determination of AA in real samples. The results clearly demonstrated that RF probe and smartphone sensing platform had excellent accuracy and reliability in detecting AA in actual samples.

In conclusion, a smartphone-integrated RF sensor was developed for visual quantitative analysis of AA in fruits using microplasma-enabled N-CDs and Rhodamine B. The N-CDs synthesized by DBD method exhibited outstanding water-solubility and fluorescence characteristics. The fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by Hg2+ through the coordinating of Hg2+ with -COOH and -NH2 on the surface of N-CDs, which belonged to DQE and SQE mechanism. AA recovered the fluorescence of the N-CDs by trapping Hg2+ from N-CDs-Hg2+ in the range of 3–170 μM with the LOD of 0.98 μM. In addition, the utilization of a smartphone platform further enhanced the practicability of AA determination, enabling visualization and quantitative analysis of AA. For visual analysis, the LOD was 2.90 μM and the linear range was 5–170 μM. The dual-signal output mode that combined fluorescence spectroscopy and visualization analysis on smartphone platform can be well applied to detect the AA content in practical juice samples. The results were consistent with the HPLC method and satisfactory recoveries were obtained. In summary, the developed method offered a speedy and simple method for visual determination of AA with remarkable accuracy and reliability. In view of the environmental hazards caused by Hg2+, we will focus more on environmentally friendly methods in our future work.

Yilan Zhao: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Xue Jiang: Methodology. Ke Huang: Methodology. Xiaoli Xiong: Supervision, Conceptualization. Qing Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22104103), and Sichuan Normal University (No. ZZYQ2023003 and KFSY2022018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.141280.
